message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/model</command-name>
            <command-message>model</command-message>
            <command-args></command-args>
<local-command-stdout>Set model to [1mOpus 4.8 (1M context)[22m and saved as your default for new sessions</local-command-stdout>

Read the file "plan.md" located in this directory. We will execute this plan under strict architectural and procedural constraints.

STRUCTURAL & TOOLS MANDATE (NON-DEBATABLE)
1. For ALL Slack API information (methods, scopes, SDK usage, Block Kit, token types), you MUST use web search exclusively on these two official Slack sources:
   - https://docs.slack.dev/
   - https://docs.slack.dev/reference/methods/
2. For ALL Maxio Advanced Billing information (API endpoints, SDK methods, subscription/component/invoice operations), you MUST use web search exclusively on these two official Maxio sources:
   - https://developers.maxio.com/
   - https://github.com/maxio-com/ab-typescript-sdk
3. You are STRICTLY FORBIDDEN from using any MCP server, context server, or any tool other than web search on the four URLs listed above. This is non-debatable.
4. Any information not found on those four URLs must be sourced only by web search targeting those same domains. Do not infer, guess, or rely on training-data knowledge for any API call shape, SDK method signature, or scope name — look it up.

PHASE 0 — SCAFFOLD FIRST (MANDATORY STOP)
Before any use case work begins, you must complete Phase 0 from plan.md as a standalone step:
- Set up npm workspaces (server/ and web/)
- Configure TypeScript for both workspaces
- Install base dependencies (Express, tsx, Vite, React, Zod, dotenv)
- Create config.ts with typed env loader
- Create .env.example with all variables from plan.md Section 7
- Create GET /api/health returning { status: "ok" }
- Ensure both servers start without errors

When Phase 0 is complete:
a. Run a compile check and confirm both server and web build without errors.
b. Stop immediately. Do not write any use case code.
c. Inform me clearly: "Phase 0 scaffold is complete. Please verify:
   1. Run `npm install` then `npm run dev`
   2. GET http://localhost:4000/api/health should return { status: 'ok' }
   3. http://localhost:5173 should load without errors
   Confirm with: 'Phase 0 verified, proceed to UC1 backend'"
d. Wait for my explicit confirmation before writing a single line of use case code.

PROCEDURAL MANDATE (USECASE-BY-USECASE LOOP)
You must implement the plan strictly ONE USECASE AT A TIME using vertical slices: for each USECASE, complete the BACKEND first, get it verified, and only then build the UI for the same feature. Never build the entire backend before starting any UI.

For each USECASE — BACKEND phase:
a. Write clean, production-ready TypeScript code for the backend (endpoint, logic, data layer).
b. Perform automated self-testing/validation to ensure the code compiles without errors.
c. Stop immediately. Do not proceed to the UI phase.
d. Inform me clearly: "USECASE [X] BACKEND is implemented and self-tested. Please manually verify via Postman." Include the exact Postman steps, the request body, and what a correct response looks like.
e. Wait for my explicit confirmation ("Okay, backend verified, proceed to the UI") before writing any UI code.

For each USECASE — UI phase (only after backend is confirmed):
a. Build clean, production-ready UI for the feature, wired against the now-stable backend contract.
b. Perform automated self-testing/validation to ensure the UI compiles without errors.
c. Stop immediately. Do not proceed to the next USECASE.
d. Inform me clearly: "USECASE [X] UI is implemented and self-tested. Please manually verify in the browser." Include exact steps: what to click, what to enter, and what the correct end-to-end result looks like.
e. Wait for my explicit confirmation ("Okay, proceed to the next USECASE") before touching any code for the next USECASE.

CODE QUALITY & STABILITY GUARANTEE
- Write production-grade, highly secure, cleanly typed TypeScript throughout.
- Ensure absolutely nothing breaks the existing codebase.
- Handle all database, network, and API errors gracefully with clear logs.
- Do not use placeholders, TODOs, or incomplete implementations.
- Every Maxio SDK method call and every Slack API call must be looked up from the official sources above before being written — not assumed from memory.

Acknowledge these constraints, confirm the four permitted URLs, read "plan.md", and begin Phase 0 scaffold only. Stop after Phase 0 and wait for my confirmation before proceeding to any use case.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- context-matic:integrate-context-matic: Discovers and integrates third-party APIs using the context-matic MCP server. Uses `fetch_api` to find available API SDKs, `ask` for integration guidance, `model_search` and `endpoint_search` for SDK details. Use when the user asks to integrate a third-party API, add an API client, implement features with an external API, or work with any third-party API or SDK.
- context-matic:onboard-context-matic: Interactive onboarding tour for the context-matic MCP server. Walks the user through what the server does, shows all available APIs, lets them pick one to explore, explains it in their project language, demonstrates model_search and endpoint_search live, and ends with a menu of things the user can ask the agent to do. USE FOR: first-time setup; "what can this MCP do?"; "show me the available APIs"; "onboard me"; "how do I use the context-matic server"; "give me a tour". DO NOT USE FOR: actually integrating an API end-to-end (use integrate-context-matic instead).
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Execute plan.md with strict architectural constraints

content.text:
I'll acknowledge the constraints and read the plan before starting.

**Constraints acknowledged:**
- ✅ Slack info: web search **only** on `https://docs.slack.dev/` and `https://docs.slack.dev/reference/methods/`
- ✅ Maxio info: web search **only** on `https://developers.maxio.com/` and `https://github.com/maxio-com/ab-typescript-sdk`
- ✅ No MCP/context servers or other tools for API info — web search on those four URLs exclusively; no inference from memory for API shapes, SDK signatures, or scope names.
- ✅ Usecase-by-usecase vertical slices: backend → verify → UI → verify, stopping for explicit confirmation each time.
- ✅ Phase 0 scaffold first, then a hard stop.
- ✅ Production-grade, secure, fully-typed TS; no placeholders/TODOs; graceful error handling.

Let me read the plan.

content.content:
1	# MeterMate — Maxio + Slack Integration Plan
2	
3	> **A two-sided billing concierge: clients book/subscribe from the frontend, Maxio runs the billing, and a private Slack channel per transaction keeps the consultant and the client in the loop from start to finish.**
4	>
5	> A **client** submits an action from a web form (book a session, subscribe, report usage, change plan, pause/cancel). The backend holds that submission in a live in-memory session, drives the matching **Maxio Advanced Billing** operation, and — this is the heart of the model — **spins up a private Slack channel scoped to that one consultant↔client transaction**, invites both parties, and narrates the transaction there with **in-progress and completion** updates.
6	>
7	> **Stack:** TypeScript · Node + Express · React (SPA) · a Maxio Advanced Billing SDK · a Slack Web API SDK
8	>
9	> **SDK-agnostic by design:** this plan specifies *what* each operation must do
10	> (create a subscription, look up a user, create a private channel, post a
11	> message), never *which* SDK method or call shape. Any supported Maxio or Slack
12	> SDK satisfies it; the implementing agent maps each capability to its SDK.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 1. Overview
17	
18	### 1.1 What this is
19	
20	MeterMate is an end-to-end billing concierge for a two-sided consulting marketplace. A client submits a billing action from the frontend; the backend drives the matching Maxio operation and narrates it live in a private Slack channel scoped to that one consultant↔client transaction.
21	
22	The design at a glance:
23	
24	| Layer | MeterMate |
25	| --- | --- |
26	| Actors | **Client** (self-serve) **+ Consultant/Admin** (hardcoded creds for now) |
27	| Input source | **React frontend** — explicit client & admin submissions |
28	| State | **in-memory session + transaction store** (live; DB-ready) |
29	| Billing engine | **Maxio Advanced Billing** — full subscription lifecycle |
30	| Notification | **a private Slack channel created per transaction**, both parties invited, live updates |
31	| Cadence | **in-progress + completion updates** posted into the transaction channel |
32	
33	### 1.2 Two actors at the frontend
34	
35	The lead confirmed both a self-subscribing **client** and a **consultant/admin** use the frontend.
36	
37	- **Client** — fills in real forms (book session, subscribe, report usage, request plan change / cancellation). No login required to *act*; identified by email.
38	- **Consultant / Admin** — for now uses **hardcoded credentials** (a simple env-configured operator identity and a guard on admin-only routes). No real signup/login flow is built in this phase; the architecture leaves a clean seam to add OAuth/JWT later. Admins can issue invoices, approve plan changes, and view the activity digest.
39	
40	### 1.3 The transaction-channel model (the core idea)
41	
42	This replaces the single shared channel with a **channel-per-transaction** topology:
43	
44	```
45	Consultant C1                        Consultant C2
46	   │                                    │
47	   ├─ Client A books C1  ─▶  #txn-c1-clienta-001   (C1 + A + bot)
48	   ├─ Client B books C1  ─▶  #txn-c1-clientb-002   (C1 + B + bot)
49	   │
50	   └─ (separate)        ─▶  Client D books C2 ─▶ #txn-c2-clientd-003 (C2 + D + bot)
51	```
52	
53	Each booking/subscription creates **one private Slack channel** containing exactly the consultant for that transaction, the client, and the MeterMate bot. The bot posts every update about *that* transaction there — and nowhere else. Different client → different channel. Different consultant → their own channels. A consultant only ever sees the channels for transactions that involve them.
54	
55	### 1.4 Why Maxio changes the use-case surface
56	
57	Maxio (formerly Chargify) is a B2B SaaS **recurring-billing platform**: Customers, Products/Families, Subscriptions (create/pause/resume/cancel/reactivate), Components (metered/quantity/prepaid/**event-based usage**), Usage records, Invoices (create/send/refund/void/credit notes), Coupons, Billing Portal, Offers, Webhooks, Insights reporting. That breadth is where the rich use-case surface comes from. We choose **depth over breadth**: five core use cases, each fully specified end-to-end.
58	
59	### 1.5 The core loop (every use case follows this shape)
60	
61	```
62	  React form (client or admin)
63	        │  POST /api/<usecase>   (+ sessionId)
64	        ▼
65	  Express route + Zod validation + (admin guard if needed)
66	        │
67	        ▼
68	  sessionStore.put / transactionStore.put         ← live memory, TTL-swept
69	        │
70	        ├─▶ slackService.ensureTxnChannel(txn)     ← create private channel +
71	        │        (create → invite consultant +         invite both parties (once)
72	        │         client → post "started")
73	        │
74	        ▼
75	  maxioService.<operation>(submission)            ← billing operation via the Maxio SDK
76	        │     │
77	        │     ├─ in-progress ─▶ slackService.postProgress(txnChannel, step)
78	        │     │
79	        │     ├─ success ─────▶ slackService.postCompletion(txnChannel, result)
80	        │     └─ failure ─────▶ slackService.postFailure(txnChannel, error)
81	        ▼
82	  JSON response back to React  { status, txnId, channelId, ...result }
83	```
84	
85	### 1.6 Pricing model (seeded, global)
86	
87	A deliberately small model: a **flat monthly retainer plan + metered usage**,
88	priced **globally** (the same rates for every consultant — the consultant is a
89	label on the transaction, not a rate). All four items are created by the Phase 1
90	seed with explicit price points, so nothing relies on Maxio defaults.
91	
92	| Item | Maxio type | Handle | Price | Scheme |
93	| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
94	| Basic plan | Product (recurring monthly) | `basic` | **$99 / month** | flat recurring |
95	| Pro plan | Product (recurring monthly) | `pro` | **$299 / month** | flat recurring |
96	| Consulting time | Metered component | `consulting-minutes` | **$2.00 / minute** | per-unit |
97	| API calls | Event-based component | `api-calls` | **$0.01 / event** | per-unit |
98	
99	- **A subscription = one plan.** UC1 enrolls the client on `basic` or `pro`; that
100	  recurring fee is the MRR shown in Slack.
101	- **Consultations bill as usage on top.** UC2 reports minutes against
102	  `consulting-minutes` (rated per-unit, accrued to the next invoice); `api-calls`
103	  covers per-event charges recorded as usage events.
104	- **Plan change** (UC3) moves `basic` ↔ `pro` with proration on the delta.
105	- **Out of scope / future:** per-consultant rates would require per-consultant
106	  products or custom price points; this phase keeps one global price point per
107	  item for a one-line seed.
108	
109	---
110	
111	## 2. Use Cases
112	
113	Five core use cases plus a cross-cutting reporting digest. Each maps to a React
114	form, an Express route, a `maxioService` operation, a transaction record, and a
115	sequence of Slack messages **inside that transaction's private channel**.
116	
117	> **Channel lifecycle, shared by all UCs:** the first action of a *new*
118	> consultant↔client pairing creates the private channel and invites both parties;
119	> subsequent actions for the same pairing **reuse** that channel (looked up in the
120	> transaction store). Every UC posts a "started" message, optional progress
121	> messages, and a "completed"/"failed" message into that channel.
122	
123	---
124	
125	### UC1 — Book & Subscribe (Client → Customer + Subscription)
126	
127	A client books a session with a named consultant and is enrolled on a plan.
128	
129	**Actor:** Client.
130	**Frontend fields:** client first/last name, client email, consultant (dropdown of consultants), product handle (plan), payment collection method (`automatic` | `remittance`), optional coupon.
131	
132	**Flow:**
133	1. React posts to `POST /api/book`.
134	2. Backend validates, creates a **transaction record** `{ txnId, consultantId, clientEmail, type: 'subscription', state: 'started' }`, caches it under the session.
135	3. `slackService.ensureTxnChannel()` creates the private channel `#txn-<consultant>-<client>-<n>`, invites the consultant + client, posts **":hourglass_flowing_sand: Booking started — creating your subscription…"**.
136	4. `maxioService.createSubscription()` **creates a subscription** for the client against the chosen plan, creating the customer inline from the submitted name/email (or reusing an existing customer matched by an email reference). The collection method and optional coupon are passed through.
137	5. On success: transaction → `completed`; post **":tada: Subscription active"** with plan, MRR, state, next assessment date, and a "View in Maxio" button.
138	6. On failure: post **":warning: Booking failed"** with the Maxio error summary; transaction → `failed`.
139	
140	**Capability required:** create a subscription (with inline customer creation, an email-based customer reference for idempotency, a payment collection method, and an optional coupon) and read back plan, MRR, state, and next assessment date.
141	
142	---
143	
144	### UC2 — Report Session Usage (Metered / Event-Based Components)
145	
146	A consultant (or client) reports consumption — e.g. consulting minutes or API
147	calls — against a metered component. Maxio rates and accrues it to the next
148	invoice.
149	
150	**Actor:** Client or Admin (both allowed).
151	**Frontend fields:** transaction reference (or consultant+client), component handle, quantity, optional memo, optional timestamp.
152	
153	**Flow:**
154	1. `POST /api/usage` → validate → resolve the existing transaction + its channel.
155	2. Post **":bar_chart: Recording usage…"** to the channel.
156	3. `maxioService.recordUsage()` **records usage** against the component: for a metered/quantity component, record a usage quantity (with optional memo); for an event-based component, record a usage event (with the optional timestamp). The service dispatches on the cached component type.
157	4. Post **":white_check_mark: Usage recorded"** with quantity, the running period total (read back from the provider's usage history), and "accrues to next invoice."
158	
159	**Capability required:** record metered usage (quantity + memo) for a component, record a usage event (timestamp) for an event-based component, and list a component's recorded usage for the current period.
160	
161	---
162	
163	### UC3 — Plan Change (Upgrade / Downgrade with Proration Preview)
164	
165	A client requests a plan change; the channel shows the prorated delta before it
166	commits.
167	
168	**Actor:** Client requests; Admin can approve (configurable).
169	**Frontend fields:** transaction reference, target product handle, timing (`prorate` | `at-renewal`).
170	
171	**Flow:**
172	1. `POST /api/plan-change/preview` → **preview the prorated cost** of moving the subscription to the target plan → returns the prorated amount to the UI **and** posts **":mag: Previewing plan change…"** + the computed delta to the channel.
173	2. On confirm: `POST /api/plan-change` → **apply the plan change**. For `prorate` timing, change the plan **now with proration** (the preview and the commit use the same prorated mechanism). For `at-renewal` timing, schedule a **non-prorated** plan change that takes effect at the next renewal.
174	3. Post **":arrows_counterclockwise: Plan changed"** old → new, proration, effective date.
175	
176	> **Proration is a capability requirement, not a default.** "Prorate now" must use
177	> a mechanism that computes and charges the prorated delta immediately *and* can
178	> be previewed beforehand (the preview must reflect the same proration the commit
179	> will apply). "At renewal" must change the plan without proration, effective next
180	> period. The agent selects whichever SDK operations provide these two behaviours;
181	> a plain attribute update that defers billing to the next period does **not**
182	> satisfy the "prorate now" path.
183	
184	---
185	
186	### UC4 — Lifecycle Control (Pause / Resume / Cancel / Reactivate)
187	
188	One form, four lifecycle actions through Maxio's subscription lifecycle operations.
189	
190	**Actor:** Client (pause/cancel own); Admin (all).
191	**Frontend fields:** transaction reference, action (`pause` | `resume` | `cancel` | `reactivate`), cancel type (`immediate` | `end-of-period`), optional reason code.
192	
193	**Flow:**
194	1. `POST /api/lifecycle` → validate → resolve transaction/channel.
195	2. Post **":vertical_traffic_light: <Action> in progress…"**.
196	3. Dispatch to the matching lifecycle operation; read back the new `state`.
197	4. Post **":vertical_traffic_light: <state-transition>"** (e.g. `active → canceled`) with reason and effective date.
198	
199	**Capability required** — map each action to the provider's matching lifecycle operation:
200	- `pause` → place the subscription on hold.
201	- `resume` → resume the on-hold subscription.
202	- `cancel` + `immediate` → cancel the subscription now.
203	- `cancel` + `end-of-period` → schedule cancellation at end of the current period (delayed cancellation).
204	- `reactivate` → reactivate a canceled subscription.
205	
206	---
207	
208	### UC5 — Invoice Issue + Send (On-Demand Billing)
209	
210	For remittance subscriptions, an **admin** issues and emails a real, itemized,
211	Maxio-hosted invoice with a hosted public payment URL.
212	
213	**Actor:** Admin only (guarded route).
214	**Frontend fields:** transaction reference, optional line items / memo, "send email" toggle.
215	
216	**Flow:**
217	1. `POST /api/invoices` (admin) → resolve transaction/channel.
218	2. Post **":receipt: Issuing invoice…"**.
219	3. **Create** the invoice (from line items / memo) → **issue** it → **optionally email** it to the client; read back the hosted public payment URL.
220	4. Post **":receipt: Invoice issued"** with amount due, due date, and a **"Pay Invoice"** button linking to the hosted invoice URL.
221	
222	**Capability required:** create an ad-hoc invoice for the subscription, issue it, optionally email it to the customer, and read the issued invoice's amount due, due date, and hosted public payment URL.
223	
224	---
225	
226	### UC6 (cross-cutting) — Billing Activity Digest
227	
228	A summary posted to a **consultant's own digest channel** (and available on a
229	manual `POST /api/digest`), built from Maxio's live data. Per-consultant, not
230	per-transaction.
231	
232	**Flow:**
233	1. Manual trigger (primary) or scheduled cron (behind a flag) → `maxioService.buildDigest(consultantId)`.
234	2. Aggregate from the provider's subscription list, invoice list, and event/activity data (active count, MRR, new signups, churn, overdue invoices) scoped to that consultant.
235	3. Post **":chart_with_upwards_trend: Billing digest"** to the consultant's digest channel.
236	
237	> **Constraint, mirrored from Maxio docs:** reporting data is for
238	> reconciliation, not real-time confirmation; counts may lag live state slightly.
239	> The digest message says so.
240	
241	**Capability required:** list subscriptions, list invoices (with status), and read recent site events/activity, each filterable to a consultant's scope.
242	
243	---
244	
245	## 3. The Slack transaction-channel mechanism
246	
247	This is the load-bearing new piece, so it is specified precisely — as
248	**capabilities and scopes**, not SDK calls. All operations below are standard
249	**bot-token** Web API capabilities — **no Enterprise/admin token required** for
250	the core path. Each maps to one method in any supported Slack SDK; the agent
251	supplies the call shape and token-passing convention its SDK requires (see §4.4).
252	
253	### 3.1 Slack capabilities used
254	
255	| Step | Capability | Scope | Notes |
256	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
257	| Resolve party → user ID | Look up a user by email | `users:read.email` | Reports "user not found" if the person isn't in the workspace |
258	| Create private channel | Create a channel, private | `groups:write` | Name ≤ 80 chars, `[a-z0-9-_]`; store both `id` and `name` |
259	| Invite both parties | Invite user(s) to the channel | `groups:write` | **Only accepts user IDs of existing workspace members**; the bot must be in the channel (it is, as creator) |
260	| Post messages | Post a message with rich blocks (Block Kit) | `chat:write` | The bot posts every update; builders return a blocks array the SDK serializes |
261	| (optional) topic/purpose | Set the channel topic | `groups:write` | Sets a human-readable transaction summary |
262	| Health check (boot) | Verify auth / identity | — | Confirms the bot token works at startup |
263	
264	### 3.2 The hard constraint, and how we handle it honestly
265	
266	**Inviting a user to a channel can only add people who already exist in the Slack
267	workspace.** Looking up a user by email is how we turn an email into a user ID,
268	and it reports "user not found" when the client isn't a workspace member. A
269	consultant's external clients usually **aren't** in the consultant's workspace,
270	and there is no supported way to add a non-member to a private channel — so
271	inviting arbitrary external clients is explicitly out of scope here (see §10).
272	
273	The plan does **not** pretend the happy path always works. `ensureTxnChannel`
274	uses a **two-tier strategy**, decided per party at runtime:
275	
276	1. **Party is a workspace member** (lookup succeeds) → invite directly. This is
277	   the clean demo path: seed the demo client + consultant as real workspace
278	   members (or single-channel guests) so invites succeed.
279	2. **Party can't be added** → the channel is still created with the consultant +
280	   bot; the client gets their updates and the hosted invoice/pay link **by email**
281	   (Maxio already emails them when an invoice is sent), and the channel notes
282	   *"client notified by email."* Nothing in the flow breaks.
283	
284	This keeps the channel-per-transaction model real and demoable while being
285	upfront that inviting arbitrary external clients into a private channel is a
286	genuine Slack limitation, not a coding detail.
287	
288	### 3.3 Channel naming & reuse
289	
290	- Name: `txn-<consultantSlug>-<clientSlug>-<seq>` (lowercased, sanitized, ≤ 80).
291	- The **transaction store** maps `(consultantId, clientEmail)` → `channelId`.
292	  The first action creates + invites; later actions for the same pair **reuse**
293	  the channel. Re-creating a channel with an existing name reports "name taken",
294	  which we treat as "look it up and reuse."
295	- Both `channelId` and `channelName` are stored (Slack docs explicitly recommend
296	  storing both; private-channel IDs can change if later shared).
297	
298	### 3.4 Update cadence (in-progress + completion)
299	
300	Every UC posts a **started** message immediately, optional **progress** messages
301	on multi-step operations (UC3 preview, UC5 create→issue→send), and a terminal
302	**completed** or **failed** message. The consultant and client watch the
303	transaction unfold live in their private channel — the "be creative" brief,
304	realized as a play-by-play rather than a single receipt.
305	
306	---
307	
308	## 4. Architecture
309	
310	### 4.1 Repository structure
311	
312	```
313	metermate/
314	├── README.md
315	├── package.json                     # npm workspaces: server + web
316	├── tsconfig.json
317	├── .env.example
318	├── .gitignore
319	│
320	├── server/                          # Express + TypeScript backend
321	│   ├── src/
322	│   │   ├── index.ts                 # bootstrap, route mounting, cron, static SPA
323	│   │   ├── config.ts                # typed env loader, constants
324	│   │   ├── auth.ts                  # hardcoded admin creds + adminGuard middleware
325	│   │   ├── stores/
326	│   │   │   ├── sessionStore.ts      # Map<sessionId, SessionData>, TTL sweep
327	│   │   │   └── transactionStore.ts  # Map<txnId,...> + (consultant,client)->channel
328	│   │   ├── maxioClient.ts           # singleton billing SDK client (whichever Maxio SDK)
329	│   │   ├── routes/
330	│   │   │   ├── book.ts              # UC1
331	│   │   │   ├── usage.ts             # UC2
332	│   │   │   ├── planChange.ts        # UC3 (+ /preview)
333	│   │   │   ├── lifecycle.ts         # UC4
334	│   │   │   ├── invoices.ts          # UC5 (adminGuard)
335	│   │   │   ├── digest.ts            # UC6
336	│   │   │   └── meta.ts              # /health, /products, /consultants
337	│   │   ├── services/
338	│   │   │   ├── maxioService.ts      # one fn per UC; no Express/Slack imports
339	│   │   │   └── slackService.ts      # ensureTxnChannel + message builders + Slack SDK
340	│   │   ├── schemas/                 # Zod request schemas per route
341	│   │   └── types.ts                 # shared domain types
342	│   └── tests/
343	│       ├── unit/                    # services, stores, builders, schemas
344	│       ├── integration/             # route -> service wiring, externals mocked
345	│       └── system/                  # supertest over HTTP, full flow
346	│
347	├── web/                             # React SPA (Vite + TypeScript)
348	│   └── src/
349	│       ├── main.tsx
350	│       ├── App.tsx                  # role switch (Client | Admin) + routing
351	│       ├── api.ts                   # typed fetch wrappers
352	│       ├── session.ts               # client-side sessionId handling
353	│       └── components/
354	│           ├── client/
355	│           │   ├── BookForm.tsx     # UC1
356	│           │   ├── UsageForm.tsx    # UC2
357	│           │   ├── PlanChangeForm.tsx  # UC3 (+ preview panel)
358	│           │   └── LifecycleForm.tsx   # UC4
359	│           └── admin/
360	│               ├── AdminLogin.tsx   # hardcoded-cred gate (simple)
361	│               ├── InvoiceForm.tsx  # UC5
362	│               └── ActivityPanel.tsx# UC6 + transaction list
363	│
364	└── docs/
365	    ├── SETUP.md                     # Maxio + Slack credentials, scopes, seeding
366	    ├── ARCHITECTURE.md              # this section expanded + diagrams
367	    ├── SLACK_CHANNELS.md            # transaction-channel mechanism + constraints
368	    ├── SERVICES.md                  # per-service contracts
369	    ├── API.md                       # REST endpoint reference
370	    ├── TESTING.md                   # test layers + AC coverage map
371	    └── USECASES.md                  # UC1–UC6 walkthroughs
372	```
373	
374	### 4.2 Component responsibilities
375	
376	| Component | Responsibility |
377	| --- | --- |
378	| `web/` (React) | Two roles (Client / Admin) selectable in the shell; one form per UC; sends `sessionId`; renders results, previews, and the resulting channel name. |
379	| `auth.ts` | Hardcoded admin identity from env; `adminGuard` middleware on UC5/UC6 and approvals. Clean seam for real auth later. |
380	| `sessionStore` | Live submission + last result per `sessionId`; TTL sweep. |
381	| `transactionStore` | Transaction records + the `(consultant,client) → channelId` map that powers channel reuse. |
382	| `maxioClient` | One billing SDK client configured with HTTP Basic auth + site + environment. |
383	| `maxioService` | One fn per UC; wraps the billing SDK, normalizes results, throws typed errors. No Express/Slack imports (unit-testable). |
384	| `slackService` | `ensureTxnChannel` (lookup→create→invite, two-tier fallback), pure message builders, message posting. |
385	| `schemas/` | Zod; invalid input → `400` before any Maxio/Slack call. |
386	
387	### 4.3 State & memory model
388	
389	- **In-memory, session + transaction scoped** (`Map`s), exactly as the lead
390	  framed it ("in memory until session exists; DB later").
391	- `sessionStore` holds the current submission + last Maxio result so multi-step
392	  flows (UC3 preview → confirm) work without re-sending everything.
393	- `transactionStore` holds transaction state and the **channel reuse map**, so the
394	  second action for a consultant↔client pair reuses the existing private channel.
395	- **TTL sweep** clears idle sessions (default 30 min) so memory doesn't grow
396	  unbounded.
397	- **Idempotency:** customer `reference` = email; a per-submission key guards
398	  double-submits so a retried form doesn't create a duplicate subscription.
399	- **DB-ready:** both stores expose `get/put/delete/sweep`; swapping to Redis/
400	  Postgres is a per-file change. Nothing else imports the `Map`s.
401	
402	### 4.4 Authentication
403	
404	- **Maxio:** HTTP Basic (API key as username, `x` as password), scoped to a
405	  **site subdomain**, **US**/**EU** environment. From `MAXIO_API_KEY`,
406	  `MAXIO_SITE_SUBDOMAIN`, `MAXIO_ENVIRONMENT`. All testing against a **Maxio test
407	  site** (test mode), never live.
408	- **Slack:** a Slack **app** with a **bot token** (`xoxb-…`) and scopes
409	  `chat:write`, `groups:write`, `users:read.email` (+ `groups:read` for reuse
410	  lookups). A bot token is required (not an incoming webhook) because the app must
411	  *create private channels and invite users* — a webhook can only post to one
412	  preconfigured channel.
413	  - **Token wiring is SDK-dependent** and abstracted behind `slackService`. Some
414	    SDKs accept the bot token directly on the client; others require additional
415	    client credentials (e.g. OAuth client id/secret) and take the token per call.
416	    The implementing agent configures whatever its chosen SDK needs; the bot token
417	    always comes from `SLACK_BOT_TOKEN`, and SETUP.md documents the exact client
418	    config for the SDK in use. Nothing outside `slackService` depends on it.
419	- **App (admin):** hardcoded operator credentials from env (`ADMIN_USER`,
420	  `ADMIN_PASSWORD`) checked by `adminGuard`. Explicitly a placeholder for real
421	  auth.
422	
423	### 4.5 Frontend ↔ backend contract
424	
425	```
426	POST /api/book                 { sessionId, firstName, lastName, email, consultantId, productHandle, collectionMethod, couponCode? }
427	POST /api/usage                { sessionId, txnRef, componentHandle, quantity, memo?, timestamp? }
428	POST /api/plan-change/preview  { sessionId, txnRef, targetHandle, timing }
429	POST /api/plan-change          { sessionId, txnRef, targetHandle, timing }
430	POST /api/lifecycle            { sessionId, txnRef, action, cancelType?, reasonCode? }
431	POST /api/invoices             { sessionId, txnRef, lineItems?, memo?, sendEmail }   (adminGuard)
432	POST /api/digest               { sessionId, consultantId, windowDays? }              (adminGuard)
433	GET  /api/health               -> { status, sessions, transactions, maxioSite, slackOk }
434	GET  /api/products             -> plan dropdown (cached at boot)
435	GET  /api/consultants          -> consultant dropdown (seeded)
436	```
437	
438	Every mutating response: `{ status: 'ok' | 'maxio_failed' | 'invalid' | 'session_expired', txnId, channelId?, channelName?, ...payload }` — a `status`-discriminated shape that always reports which channel was created or reused.
439	
440	---
441	
442	## 5. Slack message design (Block Kit)
443	
444	Every message lands **in the transaction's private channel** (UC6 digest is the
445	exception — consultant digest channel). Pattern: header + context (who/when) +
446	fields grid (billing facts) + button where relevant.
447	
448	| Moment | Header | Key fields | Button |
449	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
450	| Channel opened | `:wave: Transaction started` | consultant, client, type | — |
451	| UC1 progress | `:hourglass_flowing_sand: Creating subscription…` | plan | — |
452	| UC1 done | `:tada: Subscription active` | customer, plan, MRR, state, next bill | View in Maxio |
453	| UC2 done | `:bar_chart: Usage recorded` | component, qty, period total | — |
454	| UC3 preview | `:mag: Plan change preview` | old → new, proration | — |
455	| UC3 done | `:arrows_counterclockwise: Plan changed` | old → new, effective | View in Maxio |
456	| UC4 done | `:vertical_traffic_light: <transition>` | state, reason, date | — |
457	| UC5 done | `:receipt: Invoice issued` | amount due, due date | Pay Invoice |
458	| UC6 digest | `:chart_with_upwards_trend: Billing digest` | active, MRR, new, churn, overdue | — |
459	| any failure | `:warning: <UC> failed` | what failed, Maxio error | — |
460	
461	Builders are pure functions returning the blocks array, so they are unit-testable
462	without touching Slack.
463	
464	---
465	
466	## 6. Failure isolation
467	
468	| Failure point | Behaviour |
469	| --- | --- |
470	| Zod validation fails | `400`; no Maxio/Slack; nothing created |
471	| User lookup → not found | Fall to tier 2 (email-only); channel still created with consultant + bot |
472	| Channel create → name already taken | Reuse the existing channel (look up via store / channel listing) |
473	| Channel invite fails for one party | `:warning:` note in channel ("client notified by email"); flow continues |
474	| Maxio call fails | No completion block; failure block in channel; typed error → form |
475	| Message post fails | Logged; HTTP response still `ok` (billing is source of truth) |
476	| Session/transaction expired | `409` asking the client to restart the flow |
477	
478	**Principle:** the billing action is the source of truth; Slack
479	(channel creation, invites, messages) is notification — its failures never roll
480	back billing or block the HTTP response.
481	
482	---
483	
484	## 7. Environment variables
485	
486	```env
487	# Maxio Advanced Billing
488	MAXIO_API_KEY=
489	MAXIO_SITE_SUBDOMAIN=your-test-site
490	MAXIO_ENVIRONMENT=US                 # US | EU
491	MAXIO_DEFAULT_PRODUCT_FAMILY=
492	
493	# Slack (bot-token app)
494	SLACK_BOT_TOKEN=xoxb-...             # the app's bot token (used by slackService)
495	SLACK_DIGEST_CHANNEL=C0XXXXXXX       # fallback/consultant digest channel
496	# Plus any client credentials the chosen Slack SDK requires (e.g. OAuth client
497	# id/secret). Add them here per SETUP.md; only slackService reads them.
498	
499	# Admin (placeholder auth)
500	ADMIN_USER=admin
501	ADMIN_PASSWORD=changeme
502	
503	# App
504	PORT=4000
505	SESSION_TTL_MINUTES=30
506	DEMO_MODE=true                       # seeds demo products/components/consultants
507	DIGEST_CRON=0 9 * * 1                # ignored unless enabled
508	```
509	
510	---
511	
512	## 8. Implementation plan (phased)
513	
514	### Phase 0 — Scaffold
515	- npm workspaces: `server/` (Express + TS + tsx) and `web/` (Vite + React + TS); lint/format; `.env.example`; `config.ts`; `GET /api/health` green.
516	- **Verify:** `npm run dev` serves API on `:4000` + Vite on `:5173`; health returns `{ status: "ok" }`.
517	
518	### Phase 1 — Maxio client + test-site seeding
519	- `maxioClient.ts` (the billing SDK client); a `seed` script creating the Product Family and the four §1.6 priced items — products `basic` ($99/mo) and `pro` ($299/mo), a metered `consulting-minutes` ($2.00/min, per-unit) and an event-based `api-calls` ($0.01/event) component — plus demo consultants. Each gets an explicit price point so no Maxio default is assumed.
520	- **Verify:** seed prints handles + prices; the product list and `/api/consultants` return them.
521	
522	### Phase 2 — `slackService.ensureTxnChannel` + tiered invite + unit tests
523	- Implement lookup → create → invite with the two-tier (member-invite → email-only) fallback; channel reuse via store.
524	- **Verify (unit):** mocked Slack client — channel created, both parties invited on tier 1; a user-not-found lookup falls through to email-only without throwing; a name-already-taken result reuses the channel.
525	
526	### Phase 3 — `maxioService` (one fn per UC) + unit tests
527	- One function per UC — subscribe, record usage, preview + change plan, lifecycle, issue + send invoice, build digest; billing SDK mocked; happy + error path each.
528	- **Verify:** `npm test -- unit` green; ≥2 tests per fn.
529	
530	### Phase 4 — Block Kit builders + unit tests
531	- Pure builders per Section 5 (started/progress/completion/failure/digest).
532	- **Verify:** builder tests assert required fields + button URLs.
533	
534	### Phase 5 — Routes + stores + Zod + adminGuard
535	- `sessionStore`, `transactionStore` (with channel-reuse map); one route per UC wiring validate → store → ensureChannel → maxio → slack; `adminGuard` on UC5/UC6.
536	- **Verify (integration):** each route with Maxio + Slack mocked; assert order (started → maxio → completion), channel reuse on second action for same pair, failure isolation, admin guard blocks non-admins.
537	
538	### Phase 6 — React SPA (Client + Admin)
539	- Role switch; client forms (UC1–UC4) and admin forms (UC5–UC6); preview panel; shows resulting channel name; typed `api.ts`; `sessionId` handling.
540	- **Verify:** manual click-through both roles against the test site; a private channel appears per transaction with live updates.
541	
542	### Phase 7 — Digest cron + system tests + docs
543	- `node-cron` digest (flagged); `supertest` system tests over HTTP; write all `docs/*.md` including `SLACK_CHANNELS.md`.
544	- **Verify:** `npm test` (all layers) green; docs complete.
545	
546	---
547	
548	## 9. Testing strategy
549	
550	Three layers — unit, integration, and system — all offline in CI (no live
551	Maxio/Slack).
552	
553	- **Unit** — `maxioService` (SDK mocked); `slackService.ensureTxnChannel` tiers +
554	  builders; `sessionStore`/`transactionStore` TTL, idempotency, channel reuse;
555	  Zod schemas. One file per module.
556	- **Integration** — each Express route through to its service with the Maxio +
557	  Slack SDKs mocked; asserts validate → ensureChannel → Maxio → Slack ordering,
558	  channel **reuse** on the second action for a pair, admin guard, and that a Slack
559	  failure does not fail the HTTP response.
560	- **System (e2e)** — `supertest` against the app: real routing + stores, mocked
561	  externals; verifies the `status`-discriminated shape, the UC3 preview→confirm
562	  two-step, channel-per-transaction creation vs reuse, and session expiry.
563	- **Live smoke** — a non-CI script that runs one real call per UC against the
564	  Maxio **test site** and a scratch Slack workspace (creating a real private
565	  channel and inviting a seeded member), for manual end-to-end verification.
566	
567	### Acceptance criteria
568	
569	| ID | Criterion | UC | Priority |
570	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
571	| AC-01 | Valid booking creates customer + subscription, returns state | UC1 | must |
572	| AC-02 | A new consultant↔client pair creates exactly one private channel | UC1 | must |
573	| AC-03 | Both parties (when workspace members) are invited to the channel | UC1 | must |
574	| AC-04 | User-not-found lookup falls back gracefully; flow still completes | UC1 | must |
575	| AC-05 | Second action for the same pair reuses the existing channel | any | must |
576	| AC-06 | Invalid product handle → `maxio_failed`, failure block posted | UC1 | must |
577	| AC-07 | Duplicate booking (same email) is idempotent | UC1 | should |
578	| AC-08 | Metered usage records + accrues; event-based usage recorded as an event | UC2 | must |
579	| AC-09 | Plan-change preview returns proration before commit | UC3 | must |
580	| AC-10 | Plan change posts old→new to the transaction channel | UC3 | must |
581	| AC-11 | Each lifecycle action maps to the correct lifecycle operation | UC4 | must |
582	| AC-12 | End-of-period cancel uses delayed cancellation | UC4 | should |
583	| AC-13 | Invoice created→issued→(sent); hosted URL on Pay button | UC5 | must |
584	| AC-14 | UC5/UC6 routes reject non-admin callers (adminGuard) | UC5/6 | must |
585	| AC-15 | Started → completion messages post in order to the channel | all | must |
586	| AC-16 | Slack failure never blocks the HTTP response | all | must |
587	| AC-17 | Session/transaction expiry returns `409` | all | should |
588	| AC-18 | Every mutating route validates input before any external call | all | must |
589	
590	---
591	
592	## 10. Out of scope (mention, don't build)
593	
594	- **Real auth** — admin is hardcoded creds now; OAuth/JWT is a clean later swap.
595	- **Cross-org / external-client invites** — there is no supported way to add a
596	  non-member to a private channel, so cross-org/external-client invites are out
597	  of scope entirely. The two-tier `ensureTxnChannel` (member-invite → email-only)
598	  covers external clients via email instead.
599	- **Interactive Slack buttons / slash commands** — bot-token choice leaves this
600	  open; needs a public events endpoint; pitch point only.
601	- **Real payment-method capture** — Chargify.js / hosted Billing Portal in
602	  production; demo uses test-mode tokens.
603	- **Persistent DB** — both stores are DB-ready interfaces; Redis/Postgres later.
604	- **Maxio → app webhooks** — would make UC6 real-time instead of polled; natural
605	  follow-up.
606	- **Channel archival on transaction close** — archiving a channel could tidy
607	  completed transactions; noted, not built.
608	
609	---
610	
611	## 11. Risks & mitigations
612	
613	| Risk | Mitigation |
614	| --- | --- |
615	| External client not in Slack workspace → can't invite | Two-tier `ensureTxnChannel`: invite if member, else email-only; channel still created |
616	| Channel name collisions (name already taken) | Deterministic sanitized names + reuse map; treat a name-taken result as reuse |
617	| Private-channel ID changes if later shared | Store both `id` and `name`; re-resolve by name when needed (per Slack docs) |
618	| Slack rate limits when creating many channels | One channel per pair (reused); create only on first action; backoff on rate-limit responses |
619	| Maxio test-site rate limits | Seed once at boot; cache product/consultant lists |
620	| Component type mismatch (metered vs event) | Cache component types at startup; usage recording dispatches on type |
621	| Proration surprises on plan change | Always run preview (UC3) and show delta before commit |
622	| Missing Slack scopes (`groups:write`, `users:read.email`) | SETUP.md lists exact scopes; `/health` runs a Slack auth check at boot |
623	| Session lost on restart mid-demo | Short deterministic flows; idempotent re-submit by email reference |
624	| SDK drift | Pin the chosen Maxio and Slack SDK versions exactly |
625	
626	---
627	
628	## 12. Resolved decisions (from the lead)
629	
630	1. **Two actors:** client (self-serve forms) + admin/consultant (**hardcoded
631	   creds**, no signup/login flow this phase).
632	2. **Channel topology:** **private channel per consultant↔client transaction**,
633	   both parties invited, reused for subsequent actions of the same pair — not one
634	   shared channel.
635	3. **Cadence:** **in-progress *and* completion** updates posted into the
636	   transaction channel (play-by-play), not a passive manual-only digest. UC6
637	   digest remains a per-consultant summary, manual-trigger primary with optional
638	   cron.
639	
640	> **One honesty note carried into the build:** inviting an *external* client into
641	> a private Slack channel only works if they're already a workspace member. The
642	> two-tier fallback in §3.2 keeps the model working regardless; the clean demo
643	> path is to seed the demo client + consultant as workspace members.
644	
645	---
646	
647	_Industry: B2B SaaS recurring billing — a two-sided billing concierge, every transaction narrated in its own private Slack room._
648

file.content:
# MeterMate — Maxio + Slack Integration Plan

> **A two-sided billing concierge: clients book/subscribe from the frontend, Maxio runs the billing, and a private Slack channel per transaction keeps the consultant and the client in the loop from start to finish.**
>
> A **client** submits an action from a web form (book a session, subscribe, report usage, change plan, pause/cancel). The backend holds that submission in a live in-memory session, drives the matching **Maxio Advanced Billing** operation, and — this is the heart of the model — **spins up a private Slack channel scoped to that one consultant↔client transaction**, invites both parties, and narrates the transaction there with **in-progress and completion** updates.
>
> **Stack:** TypeScript · Node + Express · React (SPA) · a Maxio Advanced Billing SDK · a Slack Web API SDK
>
> **SDK-agnostic by design:** this plan specifies *what* each operation must do
> (create a subscription, look up a user, create a private channel, post a
> message), never *which* SDK method or call shape. Any supported Maxio or Slack
> SDK satisfies it; the implementing agent maps each capability to its SDK.

---

## 1. Overview

### 1.1 What this is

MeterMate is an end-to-end billing concierge for a two-sided consulting marketplace. A client submits a billing action from the frontend; the backend drives the matching Maxio operation and narrates it live in a private Slack channel scoped to that one consultant↔client transaction.

The design at a glance:

| Layer | MeterMate |
| --- | --- |
| Actors | **Client** (self-serve) **+ Consultant/Admin** (hardcoded creds for now) |
| Input source | **React frontend** — explicit client & admin submissions |
| State | **in-memory session + transaction store** (live; DB-ready) |
| Billing engine | **Maxio Advanced Billing** — full subscription lifecycle |
| Notification | **a private Slack channel created per transaction**, both parties invited, live updates |
| Cadence | **in-progress + completion updates** posted into the transaction channel |

### 1.2 Two actors at the frontend

The lead confirmed both a self-subscribing **client** and a **consultant/admin** use the frontend.

- **Client** — fills in real forms (book session, subscribe, report usage, request plan change / cancellation). No login required to *act*; identified by email.
- **Consultant / Admin** — for now uses **hardcoded credentials** (a simple env-configured operator identity and a guard on admin-only routes). No real signup/login flow is built in this phase; the architecture leaves a clean seam to add OAuth/JWT later. Admins can issue invoices, approve plan changes, and view the activity digest.

### 1.3 The transaction-channel model (the core idea)

This replaces the single shared channel with a **channel-per-transaction** topology:

```
Consultant C1                        Consultant C2
   │                                    │
   ├─ Client A books C1  ─▶  #txn-c1-clienta-001   (C1 + A + bot)
   ├─ Client B books C1  ─▶  #txn-c1-clientb-002   (C1 + B + bot)
   │
   └─ (separate)        ─▶  Client D books C2 ─▶ #txn-c2-clientd-003 (C2 + D + bot)
```

Each booking/subscription creates **one private Slack channel** containing exactly the consultant for that transaction, the client, and the MeterMate bot. The bot posts every update about *that* transaction there — and nowhere else. Different client → different channel. Different consultant → their own channels. A consultant only ever sees the channels for transactions that involve them.

### 1.4 Why Maxio changes the use-case surface

Maxio (formerly Chargify) is a B2B SaaS **recurring-billing platform**: Customers, Products/Families, Subscriptions (create/pause/resume/cancel/reactivate), Components (metered/quantity/prepaid/**event-based usage**), Usage records, Invoices (create/send/refund/void/credit notes), Coupons, Billing Portal, Offers, Webhooks, Insights reporting. That breadth is where the rich use-case surface comes from. We choose **depth over breadth**: five core use cases, each fully specified end-to-end.

### 1.5 The core loop (every use case follows this shape)

```
  React form (client or admin)
        │  POST /api/<usecase>   (+ sessionId)
        ▼
  Express route + Zod validation + (admin guard if needed)
        │
        ▼
  sessionStore.put / transactionStore.put         ← live memory, TTL-swept
        │
        ├─▶ slackService.ensureTxnChannel(txn)     ← create private channel +
        │        (create → invite consultant +         invite both parties (once)
        │         client → post "started")
        │
        ▼
  maxioService.<operation>(submission)            ← billing operation via the Maxio SDK
        │     │
        │     ├─ in-progress ─▶ slackService.postProgress(txnChannel, step)
        │     │
        │     ├─ success ─────▶ slackService.postCompletion(txnChannel, result)
        │     └─ failure ─────▶ slackService.postFailure(txnChannel, error)
        ▼
  JSON response back to React  { status, txnId, channelId, ...result }
```

### 1.6 Pricing model (seeded, global)

A deliberately small model: a **flat monthly retainer plan + metered usage**,
priced **globally** (the same rates for every consultant — the consultant is a
label on the transaction, not a rate). All four items are created by the Phase 1
seed with explicit price points, so nothing relies on Maxio defaults.

| Item | Maxio type | Handle | Price | Scheme |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Basic plan | Product (recurring monthly) | `basic` | **$99 / month** | flat recurring |
| Pro plan | Product (recurring monthly) | `pro` | **$299 / month** | flat recurring |
| Consulting time | Metered component | `consulting-minutes` | **$2.00 / minute** | per-unit |
| API calls | Event-based component | `api-calls` | **$0.01 / event** | per-unit |

- **A subscription = one plan.** UC1 enrolls the client on `basic` or `pro`; that
  recurring fee is the MRR shown in Slack.
- **Consultations bill as usage on top.** UC2 reports minutes against
  `consulting-minutes` (rated per-unit, accrued to the next invoice); `api-calls`
  covers per-event charges recorded as usage events.
- **Plan change** (UC3) moves `basic` ↔ `pro` with proration on the delta.
- **Out of scope / future:** per-consultant rates would require per-consultant
  products or custom price points; this phase keeps one global price point per
  item for a one-line seed.

---

## 2. Use Cases

Five core use cases plus a cross-cutting reporting digest. Each maps to a React
form, an Express route, a `maxioService` operation, a transaction record, and a
sequence of Slack messages **inside that transaction's private channel**.

> **Channel lifecycle, shared by all UCs:** the first action of a *new*
> consultant↔client pairing creates the private channel and invites both parties;
> subsequent actions for the same pairing **reuse** that channel (looked up in the
> transaction store). Every UC posts a "started" message, optional progress
> messages, and a "completed"/"failed" message into that channel.

---

### UC1 — Book & Subscribe (Client → Customer + Subscription)

A client books a session with a named consultant and is enrolled on a plan.

**Actor:** Client.
**Frontend fields:** client first/last name, client email, consultant (dropdown of consultants), product handle (plan), payment collection method (`automatic` | `remittance`), optional coupon.

**Flow:**
1. React posts to `POST /api/book`.
2. Backend validates, creates a **transaction record** `{ txnId, consultantId, clientEmail, type: 'subscription', state: 'started' }`, caches it under the session.
3. `slackService.ensureTxnChannel()` creates the private channel `#txn-<consultant>-<client>-<n>`, invites the consultant + client, posts **":hourglass_flowing_sand: Booking started — creating your subscription…"**.
4. `maxioService.createSubscription()` **creates a subscription** for the client against the chosen plan, creating the customer inline from the submitted name/email (or reusing an existing customer matched by an email reference). The collection method and optional coupon are passed through.
5. On success: transaction → `completed`; post **":tada: Subscription active"** with plan, MRR, state, next assessment date, and a "View in Maxio" button.
6. On failure: post **":warning: Booking failed"** with the Maxio error summary; transaction → `failed`.

**Capability required:** create a subscription (with inline customer creation, an email-based customer reference for idempotency, a payment collection method, and an optional coupon) and read back plan, MRR, state, and next assessment date.

---

### UC2 — Report Session Usage (Metered / Event-Based Components)

A consultant (or client) reports consumption — e.g. consulting minutes or API
calls — against a metered component. Maxio rates and accrues it to the next
invoice.

**Actor:** Client or Admin (both allowed).
**Frontend fields:** transaction reference (or consultant+client), component handle, quantity, optional memo, optional timestamp.

**Flow:**
1. `POST /api/usage` → validate → resolve the existing transaction + its channel.
2. Post **":bar_chart: Recording usage…"** to the channel.
3. `maxioService.recordUsage()` **records usage** against the component: for a metered/quantity component, record a usage quantity (with optional memo); for an event-based component, record a usage event (with the optional timestamp). The service dispatches on the cached component type.
4. Post **":white_check_mark: Usage recorded"** with quantity, the running period total (read back from the provider's usage history), and "accrues to next invoice."

**Capability required:** record metered usage (quantity + memo) for a component, record a usage event (timestamp) for an event-based component, and list a component's recorded usage for the current period.

---

### UC3 — Plan Change (Upgrade / Downgrade with Proration Preview)

A client requests a plan change; the channel shows the prorated delta before it
commits.

**Actor:** Client requests; Admin can approve (configurable).
**Frontend fields:** transaction reference, target product handle, timing (`prorate` | `at-renewal`).

**Flow:**
1. `POST /api/plan-change/preview` → **preview the prorated cost** of moving the subscription to the target plan → returns the prorated amount to the UI **and** posts **":mag: Previewing plan change…"** + the computed delta to the channel.
2. On confirm: `POST /api/plan-change` → **apply the plan change**. For `prorate` timing, change the plan **now with proration** (the preview and the commit use the same prorated mechanism). For `at-renewal` timing, schedule a **non-prorated** plan change that takes effect at the next renewal.
3. Post **":arrows_counterclockwise: Plan changed"** old → new, proration, effective date.

> **Proration is a capability requirement, not a default.** "Prorate now" must use
> a mechanism that computes and charges the prorated delta immediately *and* can
> be previewed beforehand (the preview must reflect the same proration the commit
> will apply). "At renewal" must change the plan without proration, effective next
> period. The agent selects whichever SDK operations provide these two behaviours;
> a plain attribute update that defers billing to the next period does **not**
> satisfy the "prorate now" path.

---

### UC4 — Lifecycle Control (Pause / Resume / Cancel / Reactivate)

One form, four lifecycle actions through Maxio's subscription lifecycle operations.

**Actor:** Client (pause/cancel own); Admin (all).
**Frontend fields:** transaction reference, action (`pause` | `resume` | `cancel` | `reactivate`), cancel type (`immediate` | `end-of-period`), optional reason code.

**Flow:**
1. `POST /api/lifecycle` → validate → resolve transaction/channel.
2. Post **":vertical_traffic_light: <Action> in progress…"**.
3. Dispatch to the matching lifecycle operation; read back the new `state`.
4. Post **":vertical_traffic_light: <state-transition>"** (e.g. `active → canceled`) with reason and effective date.

**Capability required** — map each action to the provider's matching lifecycle operation:
- `pause` → place the subscription on hold.
- `resume` → resume the on-hold subscription.
- `cancel` + `immediate` → cancel the subscription now.
- `cancel` + `end-of-period` → schedule cancellation at end of the current period (delayed cancellation).
- `reactivate` → reactivate a canceled subscription.

---

### UC5 — Invoice Issue + Send (On-Demand Billing)

For remittance subscriptions, an **admin** issues and emails a real, itemized,
Maxio-hosted invoice with a hosted public payment URL.

**Actor:** Admin only (guarded route).
**Frontend fields:** transaction reference, optional line items / memo, "send email" toggle.

**Flow:**
1. `POST /api/invoices` (admin) → resolve transaction/channel.
2. Post **":receipt: Issuing invoice…"**.
3. **Create** the invoice (from line items / memo) → **issue** it → **optionally email** it to the client; read back the hosted public payment URL.
4. Post **":receipt: Invoice issued"** with amount due, due date, and a **"Pay Invoice"** button linking to the hosted invoice URL.

**Capability required:** create an ad-hoc invoice for the subscription, issue it, optionally email it to the customer, and read the issued invoice's amount due, due date, and hosted public payment URL.

---

### UC6 (cross-cutting) — Billing Activity Digest

A summary posted to a **consultant's own digest channel** (and available on a
manual `POST /api/digest`), built from Maxio's live data. Per-consultant, not
per-transaction.

**Flow:**
1. Manual trigger (primary) or scheduled cron (behind a flag) → `maxioService.buildDigest(consultantId)`.
2. Aggregate from the provider's subscription list, invoice list, and event/activity data (active count, MRR, new signups, churn, overdue invoices) scoped to that consultant.
3. Post **":chart_with_upwards_trend: Billing digest"** to the consultant's digest channel.

> **Constraint, mirrored from Maxio docs:** reporting data is for
> reconciliation, not real-time confirmation; counts may lag live state slightly.
> The digest message says so.

**Capability required:** list subscriptions, list invoices (with status), and read recent site events/activity, each filterable to a consultant's scope.

---

## 3. The Slack transaction-channel mechanism

This is the load-bearing new piece, so it is specified precisely — as
**capabilities and scopes**, not SDK calls. All operations below are standard
**bot-token** Web API capabilities — **no Enterprise/admin token required** for
the core path. Each maps to one method in any supported Slack SDK; the agent
supplies the call shape and token-passing convention its SDK requires (see §4.4).

### 3.1 Slack capabilities used

| Step | Capability | Scope | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Resolve party → user ID | Look up a user by email | `users:read.email` | Reports "user not found" if the person isn't in the workspace |
| Create private channel | Create a channel, private | `groups:write` | Name ≤ 80 chars, `[a-z0-9-_]`; store both `id` and `name` |
| Invite both parties | Invite user(s) to the channel | `groups:write` | **Only accepts user IDs of existing workspace members**; the bot must be in the channel (it is, as creator) |
| Post messages | Post a message with rich blocks (Block Kit) | `chat:write` | The bot posts every update; builders return a blocks array the SDK serializes |
| (optional) topic/purpose | Set the channel topic | `groups:write` | Sets a human-readable transaction summary |
| Health check (boot) | Verify auth / identity | — | Confirms the bot token works at startup |

### 3.2 The hard constraint, and how we handle it honestly

**Inviting a user to a channel can only add people who already exist in the Slack
workspace.** Looking up a user by email is how we turn an email into a user ID,
and it reports "user not found" when the client isn't a workspace member. A
consultant's external clients usually **aren't** in the consultant's workspace,
and there is no supported way to add a non-member to a private channel — so
inviting arbitrary external clients is explicitly out of scope here (see §10).

The plan does **not** pretend the happy path always works. `ensureTxnChannel`
uses a **two-tier strategy**, decided per party at runtime:

1. **Party is a workspace member** (lookup succeeds) → invite directly. This is
   the clean demo path: seed the demo client + consultant as real workspace
   members (or single-channel guests) so invites succeed.
2. **Party can't be added** → the channel is still created with the consultant +
   bot; the client gets their updates and the hosted invoice/pay link **by email**
   (Maxio already emails them when an invoice is sent), and the channel notes
   *"client notified by email."* Nothing in the flow breaks.

This keeps the channel-per-transaction model real and demoable while being
upfront that inviting arbitrary external clients into a private channel is a
genuine Slack limitation, not a coding detail.

### 3.3 Channel naming & reuse

- Name: `txn-<consultantSlug>-<clientSlug>-<seq>` (lowercased, sanitized, ≤ 80).
- The **transaction store** maps `(consultantId, clientEmail)` → `channelId`.
  The first action creates + invites; later actions for the same pair **reuse**
  the channel. Re-creating a channel with an existing name reports "name taken",
  which we treat as "look it up and reuse."
- Both `channelId` and `channelName` are stored (Slack docs explicitly recommend
  storing both; private-channel IDs can change if later shared).

### 3.4 Update cadence (in-progress + completion)

Every UC posts a **started** message immediately, optional **progress** messages
on multi-step operations (UC3 preview, UC5 create→issue→send), and a terminal
**completed** or **failed** message. The consultant and client watch the
transaction unfold live in their private channel — the "be creative" brief,
realized as a play-by-play rather than a single receipt.

---

## 4. Architecture

### 4.1 Repository structure

```
metermate/
├── README.md
├── package.json                     # npm workspaces: server + web
├── tsconfig.json

├── .gitignore
│

│   ├── src/

│   │   ├── config.ts                # typed env loader, constants
│   │   ├── auth.ts                  # hardcoded admin creds + adminGuard middleware
│   │   ├── stores/
│   │   │   ├── sessionStore.ts      # Map<sessionId, SessionData>, TTL sweep
│   │   │   └── transactionStore.ts  # Map<txnId,...> + (consultant,client)->channel
│   │   ├── maxioClient.ts           # singleton billing SDK client (whichever Maxio SDK)

│   │   │   └── slackService.ts      # ensureTxnChannel + message builders + Slack SDK
│   │   ├── schemas/                 # Zod request schemas per route
│   │   └── types.ts                 # shared domain types
│   └── tests/

│       ├── integration/             # route -> service wiring, externals mocked
│       └── system/                  # supertest over HTTP, full flow
│
├── web/                             # React SPA (Vite + TypeScript)
│   └── src/
│       ├── main.tsx

│       ├── api.ts                   # typed fetch wrappers

│       └── components/

│           │   ├── UsageForm.tsx    # UC2
│           │   ├── PlanChangeForm.tsx  # UC3 (+ preview panel)
│           │   └── LifecycleForm.tsx   # UC4

│

    ├── ARCHITECTURE.md              # this section expanded + diagrams

    ├── SERVICES.md                  # per-service contracts

```

### 4.2 Component responsibilities

| Component | Responsibility |
| --- | --- |
| `web/` (React) | Two roles (Client / Admin) selectable in the shell; one form per UC; sends `sessionId`; renders results, previews, and the resulting channel name. |
| `auth.ts` | Hardcoded admin identity from env; `adminGuard` middleware on UC5/UC6 and approvals. Clean seam for real auth later. |
| `sessionStore` | Live submission + last result per `sessionId`; TTL sweep. |
| `transactionStore` | Transaction records + the `(consultant,client) → channelId` map that powers channel reuse. |
| `maxioClient` | One billing SDK client configured with HTTP Basic auth + site + environment. |
| `maxioService` | One fn per UC; wraps the billing SDK, normalizes results, throws typed errors. No Express/Slack imports (unit-testable). |
| `slackService` | `ensureTxnChannel` (lookup→create→invite, two-tier fallback), pure message builders, message posting. |
| `schemas/` | Zod; invalid input → `400` before any Maxio/Slack call. |

### 4.3 State & memory model

- **In-memory, session + transaction scoped** (`Map`s), exactly as the lead
  framed it ("in memory until session exists; DB later").
- `sessionStore` holds the current submission + last Maxio result so multi-step
  flows (UC3 preview → confirm) work without re-sending everything.
- `transactionStore` holds transaction state and the **channel reuse map**, so the
  second action for a consultant↔client pair reuses the existing private channel.
- **TTL sweep** clears idle sessions (default 30 min) so memory doesn't grow
  unbounded.
- **Idempotency:** customer `reference` = email; a per-submission key guards
  double-submits so a retried form doesn't create a duplicate subscription.
- **DB-ready:** both stores expose `get/put/delete/sweep`; swapping to Redis/
  Postgres is a per-file change. Nothing else imports the `Map`s.

### 4.4 Authentication

- **Maxio:** HTTP Basic (API key as username, `x` as password), scoped to a
  **site subdomain**, **US**/**EU** environment. From `MAXIO_API_KEY`,
  `MAXIO_SITE_SUBDOMAIN`, `MAXIO_ENVIRONMENT`. All testing against a **Maxio test
  site** (test mode), never live.
- **Slack:** a Slack **app** with a **bot token** (`xoxb-…`) and scopes
  `chat:write`, `groups:write`, `users:read.email` (+ `groups:read` for reuse
  lookups). A bot token is required (not an incoming webhook) because the app must
  *create private channels and invite users* — a webhook can only post to one
  preconfigured channel.
  - **Token wiring is SDK-dependent** and abstracted behind `slackService`. Some
    SDKs accept the bot token directly on the client; others require additional
    client credentials (e.g. OAuth client id/secret) and take the token per call.
    The implementing agent configures whatever its chosen SDK needs; the bot token
    always comes from `SLACK_BOT_TOKEN`, and SETUP.md documents the exact client
    config for the SDK in use. Nothing outside `slackService` depends on it.
- **App (admin):** hardcoded operator credentials from env (`ADMIN_USER`,
  `ADMIN_PASSWORD`) checked by `adminGuard`. Explicitly a placeholder for real
  auth.

### 4.5 Frontend ↔ backend contract

```
POST /api/book                 { sessionId, firstName, lastName, email, consultantId, productHandle, collectionMethod, couponCode? }
POST /api/usage                { sessionId, txnRef, componentHandle, quantity, memo?, timestamp? }
POST /api/plan-change/preview  { sessionId, txnRef, targetHandle, timing }
POST /api/plan-change          { sessionId, txnRef, targetHandle, timing }
POST /api/lifecycle            { sessionId, txnRef, action, cancelType?, reasonCode? }
POST /api/invoices             { sessionId, txnRef, lineItems?, memo?, sendEmail }   (adminGuard)
POST /api/digest               { sessionId, consultantId, windowDays? }              (adminGuard)
GET  /api/health               -> { status, sessions, transactions, maxioSite, slackOk }
GET  /api/products             -> plan dropdown (cached at boot)
GET  /api/consultants          -> consultant dropdown (seeded)
```

Every mutating response: `{ status: 'ok' | 'maxio_failed' | 'invalid' | 'session_expired', txnId, channelId?, channelName?, ...payload }` — a `status`-discriminated shape that always reports which channel was created or reused.

---

## 5. Slack message design (Block Kit)

Every message lands **in the transaction's private channel** (UC6 digest is the
exception — consultant digest channel). Pattern: header + context (who/when) +
fields grid (billing facts) + button where relevant.

| Moment | Header | Key fields | Button |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Channel opened | `:wave: Transaction started` | consultant, client, type | — |
| UC1 progress | `:hourglass_flowing_sand: Creating subscription…` | plan | — |
| UC1 done | `:tada: Subscription active` | customer, plan, MRR, state, next bill | View in Maxio |
| UC2 done | `:bar_chart: Usage recorded` | component, qty, period total | — |
| UC3 preview | `:mag: Plan change preview` | old → new, proration | — |
| UC3 done | `:arrows_counterclockwise: Plan changed` | old → new, effective | View in Maxio |
| UC4 done | `:vertical_traffic_light: <transition>` | state, reason, date | — |
| UC5 done | `:receipt: Invoice issued` | amount due, due date | Pay Invoice |
| UC6 digest | `:chart_with_upwards_trend: Billing digest` | active, MRR, new, churn, overdue | — |
| any failure | `:warning: <UC> failed` | what failed, Maxio error | — |

Builders are pure functions returning the blocks array, so they are unit-testable
without touching Slack.

---

## 6. Failure isolation

| Failure point | Behaviour |
| --- | --- |
| Zod validation fails | `400`; no Maxio/Slack; nothing created |
| User lookup → not found | Fall to tier 2 (email-only); channel still created with consultant + bot |
| Channel create → name already taken | Reuse the existing channel (look up via store / channel listing) |
| Channel invite fails for one party | `:warning:` note in channel ("client notified by email"); flow continues |
| Maxio call fails | No completion block; failure block in channel; typed error → form |
| Message post fails | Logged; HTTP response still `ok` (billing is source of truth) |
| Session/transaction expired | `409` asking the client to restart the flow |

**Principle:** the billing action is the source of truth; Slack
(channel creation, invites, messages) is notification — its failures never roll
back billing or block the HTTP response.

---
